blurb: A slow, chalk-white column of tightly stacked leaves covered in a fine warty bloom, packed so densely the stem all but disappears. A collector's miniature that wants sun and almost no water.

care.light: Full sun to very bright light keeps the columns tight, white, and compact. Weak light makes it stretch and lose the neat stacked look.
care.water: Water sparingly and only when bone dry. Like most Crassula it grows in the cool season and largely rests through summer heat — keep it especially dry then to avoid rot.
care.soil: A sharp, mostly-mineral mix (heavy on pumice or grit); it will not tolerate staying damp.
care.temperature: Room temperature; keep above ~40°F and dry when cold.
care.dormancy: A winter grower — most active in the cooler, shorter days and dormant in peak summer, which is the opposite of many succulents.
care.feeding: Barely feeds; a very dilute feed once in the growing season at most.
care.potting: Slow-growing and happy snug; repot infrequently into fresh gritty mix.
care.propagation: From stem cuttings or leaves, allowed to callus before setting on dry mix.
care.bloom: Small pale flowers cluster at the stem tips, appearing mainly in winter.
care.troubles: Summer rot from warmth-plus-water is the classic mistake; mealybugs otherwise.
care.toxicity: Like other Crassula, considered mildly toxic to cats and dogs if eaten.
